73701.subject: SoFi, Cash App, Mastercard: Business in Front, Blockchain in Back
73701.body:
**gm, Defiers!**

These were our top news today:

* **[SoFi](https://thedefiant.io/news/tradfi-and-fintech/sofi-brings-its-bank-issued-stablecoin-to-14-7-million-members)**[ rolls out bank-issued stablecoin](https://thedefiant.io/news/tradfi-and-fintech/sofi-brings-its-bank-issued-stablecoin-to-14-7-million-members)

* **[Mastercard](https://thedefiant.io/news/tradfi-and-fintech/mastercard-wins-ny-bitlicense-deepening-push-into-stablecoin-settlement)**[ wins NY BitLicense](https://thedefiant.io/news/tradfi-and-fintech/mastercard-wins-ny-bitlicense-deepening-push-into-stablecoin-settlement)

* [Cash App adds fee-free USDC transfers](https://thedefiant.io/news/tradfi-and-fintech/cash-app-goes-live-with-fee-free-usdc-transfers-framing-stablecoins-as-a-path-to-bitcoin)

* **[DTCC](https://thedefiant.io/news/tradfi-and-fintech/dtcc-picks-stellar-for-tokenized-securities-rollout-as-multi-chain-push-expands)**[ picks Stellar for tokenized securities](https://thedefiant.io/news/tradfi-and-fintech/dtcc-picks-stellar-for-tokenized-securities-rollout-as-multi-chain-push-expands)

* **[OpenZeppelin](https://thedefiant.io/news/hacks/openzeppelin-pushes-back-after-ex-cto-declares-all-of-defi-unsafe)**[ pushes back on ex-CTO](https://thedefiant.io/news/hacks/openzeppelin-pushes-back-after-ex-cto-declares-all-of-defi-unsafe)

Ethereum's soul-searching, the hype around HYPE, and BTC's price aside, the biggest story in the blockchain industry continues to be stablecoins. Today's headlines proved it, with three major fintech and payment players rolling out digital-dollar infrastructure.

Stablecoins are coming to mom-and-pop, whether they know it or not. Just today, SoFi opened SoFiUSD to its 14.7 million app members — the first time a US bank-issued stablecoin has been available within a consumer banking interface; Mastercard secured a New York BitLicense, signaling it's taking meaningful steps to settle payments onchain; and Cash App began rolling out USDC to its ~50 million users.

What's still TBD is how these players will position their product to fit the [Clarity Act's specific design](https://thedefiant.io/news/regulation/latest-clarity-act-draft-bans-rewards-on-passive-stablecoin-balances): the current draft bans rewards "economically equivalent to interest" on passive stablecoin balances and explicitly allows activity-based rewards. Consumer fintechs will have to fill that gap with cashback, usage rebates, and merchant-funded promotions.

Different strategies are already taking shape. PayPal issued PYUSD with Paxos and went the cross-chain, merchant-and-developer route, [crossing $4 billion in supply by February](https://thedefiant.io/news/tradfi-and-fintech/paypal-pyusd-supply-crosses-usd4-billion). SoFi is taking the bank-charter route: vertical integration, consumer-first deployment, FDIC-adjacent positioning. 

Stripe is playing a different game entirely: infrastructure. After acquiring stablecoin orchestration firm Bridge for $1.

73698.subject: 🟪 Disclosure is all we need
73698.body:
View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/3f889764-4c4c-426b-b0fe-da4071761d08/BD-Banner-Sponsor_Template.png?t=1779483553)
Follow image link: (https://ore.supply/)
Caption: 

View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/fa8d928f-f7a0-41bb-ad3c-645522c72e30/Quote_template_-_Breakdown_NL.png?t=1779918661)
Caption: 

View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/49c1a0dc-4e36-428d-96f7-c0b77db6a2f4/image.png?t=1779918144)
Caption: 

# **Disclosure is all we need**

Columbia law professor Tim Wu [argues](https://pca.st/episode/1c4b9293-0a37-403f-a2eb-834687b184aa?t=1037) that every kind of economic transaction relies first and foremost on buyers feeling informed. People want to know that what they’re buying is of reasonable quality, that the price is approximately right, that they’re not being bamboozled. In Wu’s telling, intermediaries that aggregate information and create trust between strangers emerge to solve this problem, making transactions possible at scale. EBay, for example, is a good place to buy high-priced baseball cards because of all the information it offers on sellers.

“Probably the most successful example is the stock market,” Wu says, “which gives people enough comfort that they’re willing to buy what is basically a piece of paper that supposedly says you own part of a company.”

Why do we trust these pieces of paper? It's not, as you might assume, the law. Shareholders only have legal rights of ownership in cases of bankruptcy and takeovers, so Wu is correct to cast aspersions on the idea that owning shares makes you part owner of a company. 

If anything, a close reading of corporate law should make us _dis_trust the stock market. Thanks largely to the [business judgement rule](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Business_judgment_rule), company executives are free to do just about whatever they want with the capital we give them 

But we’ve come to trust them to do the right thing — to treat us _as if_ we own the company. 

This was a process that took many decades. And it began with disclosures.

In 1899, a congressional investigator [asked](https://archive.org/details/cu31924092545411/page/122/mode/2up?q=blindly) Henry O. Havemeyer, the robber baron head of the Sugar Trust, whether investors had any right to know what they were buying on the stock market. His response captured the spirit of 19th-century investing:

Commissioner: “You think then, that when a corporation chartered by the State offers stock to the public, and is one in which the public is interested, that the public has no right to know what its earning power is or to subject them to any inspection whatever, that the people may not buy stock blindly?”

Havemeyer: “Yes, that is my theory. Let the b

73696.subject: How Can You Save Millions of Claude Tokens?
73696.body:
The Atlas

Автор: GenAI Works


Автор: GenAI Works

    
    
    Прочитайте цю статтю на LinkedIn, щоб приєднатися до обговорення
Прочитати на LinkedIn
https://www.linkedin.com/comm/pulse/how-can-you-save-millions-claude-tokens-genai-works-mbboe?lipi=urn%3Ali%3Apage%3Aemail_email_series_follow_newsletter_02%3BlOUrt0JiQP6l4BraUvgRkQ%3D%3D&midToken=AQFTjFOONAfT2w&midSig=0HfWVB7Fg2Gcg1&trk=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_&trkEmail=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_-null-fz96fz~mpok90l6~js-null-null&eid=fz96fz-mpok90l6-js
  
Welcome back! This week is about one clear shift: AI teams are becoming more efficient, more structured, and more agentic. Claude’s prompt caching shows how developers can save hundreds of millions of tokens. Code with Claude London shows why AI learning needs a clear path, not just a playlist. Anthropic’s team shares practical prompting lessons from real debugging. And Google’s agent skills repo shows how agents can get the right knowledge at the right time. In today's Atlas Newsletter: Prompt caching in Claude: how one feature saved 300 million tokens in a week without changing a single setting? Code with Claude London: the 24-video playlist mapped into a learning path from model basics to enterprise scale. Anthropic's prompting playbook: six lessons from a live debugging session that beats most paid courses. Google's agent skills repo: why the next wave of AI performance may come from context architecture rather than bigger models? PS: Want to share your AI tool or company news with our 14M+ community? Get in touch. How to save millions of Claude Tokens in 10 minutes? Claude has a feature that can save developers millions of tokens a week. One creator reportedly saved 91 million tokens in a single day and more than 300 million in a week without changing a single setting. That is prompt caching working as intended. The idea is simple: Claude does not need to reprocess the same context every time. Cached tokens cost around 10% of normal input tokens. So if Claude reads 91 million cached tokens, the effective cost is closer to 9 million. That is the difference between burning context repeatedly and reusing it intelligently. Claude’s cache is organized in layers: System layer: base instructions, tools, and output style. Project layer: CLAUDE.md, memory, and project rules. Conversation layer: messages and replies rebuilt each turn. The problem is that many users accidentally break the cache. → Switch models mid-session? Cache resets. → Let a session sit idle for more than an hour? Cache goes cold. → Use sub-agents heavily? Their cache window is much shorter. → Run /compact or /clear? You reset the cache. This matters because high cache hit rates reduce cost, improve speed, and make session limits feel much more generous. Want the three habits that help most users save tokens and keep Claude sessions efficient? Subscribe here. Don’t miss it From data sto
